text: As a conductor of 14 years  and going into some major city's in the United States. that having drones flying over your head while trying to work as have no idea if it is a company drone a rail fan drone or maybe a drone of someone planning terrorist activities is very distracting! As we deal with cars carrying some of the most hazardous stuff out there and wondering if somebody is watching to figure out how to create a toxic situation is very concerning and believe if we can keep drones out of any transportation would be a great help to national security and a great help to the people who have to work in these situations. Thank you for your time in listening to these concerns!

I think they are very dangerous to us when they use them to test us it takes our mind off doing our work worrying about looking up wondering if you’re being watched in some cases they are good to use for instance if there is a derail and they can’t into places

I am writing to provide public comment on the proposed rule regarding the restriction of drone operations around transportation facilities and rail infrastructure. My name is Matthew Hampton, and I have 18 years of experience as a conductor and engineer.

Active rail yards are inherently dangerous environments that require total concentration. Drones overhead create unnecessary distractions for crews, and their use for surveillance and operational testing introduces serious hazards and security concerns. Furthermore, normalizing drone flights over rail cars carrying thousands of gallons of hazardous chemicals is a significant risk; it becomes impossible for workers to distinguish between an official operational test and a potential act of terrorism in progress.

I have personally witnessed Management (MTOs) violate existing FAA guidelines by flying drones near airports in Evansville and Avon, Indiana. When questioned, these individuals claimed to be unaware of the nearby airports, despite the constant presence of low-flying jets. Additionally, drones often cause conductors to lose focus during critical switching operations, and I have even been told by management that they have lost control of drones due to signal failure.

Frontline railroad workers are the true experts on rail safety. We deserve to be consulted before these practices are normalized, and I strongly support the implementation of stricter regulations to ensure our safety and the security of our nation's infrastructure.

Matthew Hampton

I am a 29 year railroad conductor. I work in a rail yard near an airport. Planes fly over the rail yard at low altitude. Managers fly drones at high altitude to test workers. Its a recipe for disaster.

s an aviation student and future pilot, I appreciate the FAA's effort to implement a long-overdue framework for restricting unauthorized drone operations near critical infrastructure. The proposed UAFR system provides much-needed regulatory clarity for both facility operators and drone pilots, and the security concerns motivating this rule including hundreds of documented drone incidents at correctional facilities and unauthorized surveillance of energy sites clearly justify action.
That said, I have two concerns. First, with approximately 125,000 facilities potentially qualifying for restrictions across 16 sectors, the cumulative impact on low-altitude airspace could be significant for commercial operators and delivery services. Second, sector-specific criteria for several facility types remain undefined. The FAA should finalize criteria for all 16 sectors before issuing a final rule to avoid an unpredictable regulatory environment.
Overall, I support this rule making, but urge the FAA to ensure all active UAFRs are integrated into real-time airspace tools like LAANC, and that eligibility criteria are complete and clearly defined before the rule takes effect.

would like to provide my first-hand experience with the carrier I work for (CSX) flying drones in active rail yards, and reasons why in my opinion, this practice should be federally banned. I hope this explanation helps the union's cause in banning drone operations in rail yards, coming from a front-line employee that has to deal with this.

The first reason being the drones the carrier operates are strictly used for malice against employees from the management, looking to capture camera footage of minuscle rules violations and never to improve operational efficiency or safety. I have a first hand experience to testify this. In the summer of 2024, I was the engineer on a southbound loaded auto rack train that had to drop cars in Bellmen's yard near North Bergen, NJ. We pulled in the north end of the yard at CP 5, and the conductor was instructed to get off the train there and stay at the north end to make sure the cars were in the clear and retrieve the EOT. When I started pulling and got to the south end, we had misaligned switches, so I stopped the train and informed the conductor the switches were wrong. While sitting there, I spotted an MTO's company vehicle with the MTO outside with a remote control, meaning they have a drone up watching us. I also was able to spot the drone almost directly above the train hovering above the misaligned switches. Now the carrier will tell you that they never fly drones directly above employees, but I have seen them do this many times. They do that to capture the best quality camera footage they can for the investigation when they try to discipline us for miniscule operating rule violations. The MTO knew the conductor was at the opposite end of the yard and asked me if would get the switches. This MTO has a reputation for malice and knowing that he was out looking for blood, I refused to get the switches and he didn't push the issue. Instead of taking the drone down and driving back to the opposite end of the yard to pick up the conductor, he just sat outside with the drone and made the conductor wait for an hour and a half for the conductor mentor to come pick him up and drive him down to the switches. Meanwhile, our train was strung out, blocking two public road crossings on busy streets during rush hour in New Jersey this entire time. You would think while that MTO had the drone up, he would have seen the switches were misaligned and told the conductor to stay on the head end instead of getting off at the opposite end of the yard. He could have also taken the drone down and picked the conductor up to get the switches, but he couldn't be bothered because that would have interfered with him trying to capture rules violations on the drone's camera footage. This goes to show that the carrier strictly uses drones for malicious employee surveillance and never to improve operational efficiency or safety. He did end up writing the conductor up for a miniscule violation and was there to nit pick rules and not help us with our work in any way, seeing as he made the conductor wait an hour and a half for a ride. I have seen the carrier use drones probably 100 times, and never once did they use them for anything else besides employee surveillance. Never once have I ever seen them used to improve operational efficiency, or improve safety, such as informing us of potential hazards pertaining to our work, like misaligned switches ahead of the train's movement.

The second issue is that when we spot drones, we have no way of knowing whether or not they are being flown by railroad management or someone else, since they never tell us when they are using drones to spy on us. That would blow their cover. Rail buffs often fly unauthorized drones over rail yards to look at the trains. If they can get away with this, then so could a potential terrorist looking to scout out the terminal to orchestrate an act of terrorism. I am sure rail buffs aren't supposed to fly dones over rail yards, but there aren't ever any authorities around to ever enforce this. This shows that anybody can do that and get away with it. Banning drones flying over active rail yards would eliminate that potential threat, because any time we spot a drone flying over the yard, we could report it to the proper authorities knowing that it's not supposed to be there.

To address the drone use I don’t think it’s right by law and as a employee I take my job serious cause this is how I feed my son their just looking for anything to fire people for and they target certain people most being new hires the old heads they let them get away with murder. I vote to get rid of the drones

Drones do nothing but take my focus off of the work I do, to make sure I'm constantly looking up in the sky. That's not safe at all for ke considering I work in close proximity to massive, steel, deadly rolling equipment. End this nonsense, for us and our safety, please!

23 years as a switchman in a hump yard. I spend my days avoiding getting hit by free rolling box cars traveling at up to 15 mph. These yards are hazardous enough, now I’m getting buzzed by drones. They are crazy distracting and honestly very disturbing. With the threat level in this great country being what it is. I don’t know what these drones are doing or who is running them. Drones in rail yards are a dangerous game. I can’t believe we haven’t had any more injuries than we have.

Dear Federal Aviation Administration,
I am a 50-year-old transportation professional with over 30 years of hands-on experience working across Michigan. I have worked with crews handling hazardous materials and all types of rolling stock in real-world conditions. I urge the FAA to explicitly prohibit the use of commercial drones for observing, monitoring, or disciplining employees in workplaces, especially in transportation and industrial settings.
Using drones to watch crews is not about safety or efficiency — it is purely a tool for discipline and control. Companies will claim otherwise, but experienced workers know the truth. This practice creates serious safety hazards that the FAA must not enable.
Deadly Distraction and Reduced Situational Awareness
In our industry, absolute concentration is required at all times. We work around moving equipment, hazardous materials, heavy rolling stock, rail yards, loading docks, and busy transportation corridors. Any distraction can lead to catastrophe.
Drone surveillance makes employees paranoid and nervous. Instead of focusing on the dangerous tasks in front of them, workers will be constantly looking up, wondering if they are being watched or recorded. This divided attention is deadly. It increases the likelihood of serious incidents that can harm not only employees, but also neighbors, nearby businesses, and the general public.
Unnecessary Added Risk in Already Complex Environments
The skies above our work sites are already busy. Introducing persistent drone flights for employee monitoring adds collision risks, distraction hazards, and the potential for drones to crash or lose control over populated or operational areas. Companies already have extensive ground-based surveillance cameras. Those systems are intrusive enough and should themselves be reviewed for safety and privacy impacts in the future. There is no justification for layering aerial surveillance on top of them.
From my decades of experience, I can tell you that the safest crews are the ones who feel trusted to focus on their jobs, not the ones who feel they are being spied on from above. Paranoia does not improve safety — it destroys it.
FAA’s Responsibility: Prioritize Aviation and Public Safety
The FAA’s mission is to ensure the safe and efficient use of the National Airspace System. Approving routine drone flights whose main purpose is employee discipline would directly contradict that mission. These operations would place unnecessary stress on workers in high-hazard environments and expose surrounding communities to added risk.
I respectfully request that the FAA ban the use of drones (especially BVLOS and loitering operations) for employee monitoring, rule enforcement, or disciplinary purposes. Any commercial drone approvals in transportation or industrial areas should strictly prohibit surveillance of personnel.
Please protect workers, the public, and airspace safety by rejecting corporate attempts to turn drones into tools for workplace spying. Do not let companies fool regulators with claims of “improved oversight.” Experience shows this only creates more danger.
Thank you for your attention to this critical issue.
Sincerely,
Matt Delaney
Transportation Professional with 30+ years of experience in Michigan

I am not in support of the use of drones in or near rail yards.  The safety of the railroad employees should take priority over the railroad companies interest in spying on and testing their individual employees. The employees already have enough to worry about while performing their duties safely. The use of drones is a huge distraction to the employees. By having this extra distraction, many bad things could happen resulting in something as little as a minor injuries or major incidents resulting in death. When the railroad companies perform drone testing they don’t notify the railroad employees of such events, resulting in very awkward situations. The railroad employee is never certain if the done is being used for testing or some kind of outside terrorist threat. How is the individual supposed to know with this unidentified aircraft flying over head?  There is also a concern for the general public with the use of these drones. The general public experiences many of the same concerns. They could easily become distracted while drones are in the area causing a wreck or lose their focus while performing a task which involves moving heavy equipment resulting in injury or even death.

I was a rail worker who was injured on the job in 2022. I have continued working up till march 2026. While working in Montpelier Idaho management at Union Pacific observed me for over 90 minutes with a drone. Do to the previously mentioned on duty injury I was haveing trouble walking, my back is seriously injured. Jon Smith was only interested in finding a rule violation and either chose to over look how I was having trouble walking or is ignorant. Drones don’t do anything for safety only hinder our ability to work safely or efficiently.

Comment on Docket FAA 2026 4558
Designation Restricting the Operation of
Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed
Site Facility
Section 2209 NPRM
I am a drone operator based in Temple, Texas. I support the genuine protection of critical
infrastructure, national security facilities, and high risk sites. However, I urge the FAA to
withdraw this proposed rule making and pursue a more narrowly tailored and security
focused approach.
Core Position
The proposed expansion of UAS flight restrictions around fixed site facilities would create
broad geographic restrictions without addressing one of the most significant long term
vulnerabilities in the domestic UAS ecosystem: the continued use of insecure foreign origin
drones and components in United States airspace.
The United States already has systems for responding to domestic threats, law enforcement
concerns, and facility specific risks. The greater strategic danger is not only the physical
misuse of drones, but also the data, supply chain, surveillance, and economic security risks
created by foreign origin systems, particularly those tied to adversarial nations.
If the national concern is security, then the policy response should focus on trusted aircraft,
trusted components, and trusted supply chains. A phased national policy prioritizing NDAA
compliant drones, including secure design, manufacturing, assembly, and component
sourcing, would do more to protect the country than creating hundreds or thousands of new
restricted zones across the low altitude airspace.
Categorical restrictions should remain limited to DOD and DOE facilities already covered by
established national security restrictions unless Congress specifically authorizes additional
categories, defines them narrowly by statute, and requires periodic review. Other facilities
should not be allowed to broadly restrict lawful access to the National Airspace System when
safer, more precise security measures are available.
Analysis
This proposed rule risks creating a framework where fixed site operators can restrict lawful
UAS activity around their facilities while insecure foreign origin drones remain widely
available and operational throughout the country. That is the wrong security tradeoff.
The rule would also fragment low altitude airspace and make commercial drone operations
more difficult, more expensive, and less scalable. Inspections, mapping, documentation,
thermal imaging, LiDAR capture, infrastructure assessment, emergency response support,

and future delivery operations all depend on predictable access to airspace. Broad fixed site
restrictions would create operational uncertainty for lawful American operators while doing
little to address supply chain based security risks.
The United States should not respond to legitimate infrastructure concerns by broadly
restricting American citizens and American businesses from using domestic airspace.
Airspace security should be based on actual risk, trusted technology, and clearly defined
national security needs, not broad geographic exclusion.
The FAA should also recognize that UAS technology is rapidly becoming essential for
property assessment, emergency documentation, disaster response, search and rescue,
maintenance, and public safety. Overly broad UAS flight restrictions may unintentionally slow
the growth of secure domestic capability by making lawful operations more complex, less
predictable, and more expensive. Automated systems and dock based services can be
valuable parts of the future UAS ecosystem, but they should compete on capability and
safety, not become the only practical option because broad airspace restrictions make
smaller lawful operators unworkable.
Recommendations
1. Withdraw the proposed rule making in its current form.
2. Replace broad fixed site UAS restriction authority with a narrower framework limited to
clearly defined national security and critical risk facilities.
3. Rather than relying primarily on broad geographic UAS flight restrictions, the FAA should
coordinate with Congress, DHS, DOD, DOE, and other federal partners to expand the
existing secure drone policy framework beyond federal procurement and federally
funded operations, with the long term goal of removing untrusted foreign drones and
components from sensitive U.S. airspace.
4. Maintain existing protections for DOD and DOE facilities already covered by established
national security restrictions. Do not create additional restricted facility categories unless
Congress specifically authorizes them, defines them narrowly, and requires periodic
review.
5. Provide a temporary exemption for public safety, search and rescue, disaster response,
and emergency response UAS operations from any new fixed site restrictions when no
secure domestic platform can reasonably meet the mission requirement. This exemption
should be time limited, reviewed regularly, and paired with federal grants, procurement
incentives, tax credits, and domestic manufacturing support to accelerate American
made UAS capabilities for operations in remote, mountainous, inhospitable, or
communications degraded environments. The policy goal should be clear: preserve life
saving capability today while rapidly eliminating long term dependence on untrusted
foreign platforms.
6. Establish a distinct regulatory pathway for physically constrained, closed circuit, or
tethered UAS systems used as industrial work augmentation tools. These systems should
have clear ways to remain within non data capturing frameworks when configured to
avoid unnecessary imaging, mapping, transmission, retention, or reporting of data.

Properly structured, this category can expand the use of UAS for spraying, cutting,
painting, lifting, cleaning, inspection support, and similar work while reducing American
worker exposure to hazardous heights, chemicals, unstable structures, confined access
areas, and other dangerous traditional work environments.
7. Require any future fixed site restriction process

Drones over railroad yards create distractions. They provide no tangible use other than keeping MTO's from sitting in the weeds to nit pick operations. Conductors and engineers are proven professionals and know their crafts and for years the railroads have disregarded this fact. Due to the railroads ultimate goal of eliminating our crafts, they have implemented this tactic which adds to the hazards that face us on a daily basis. It has become blatantly obvious to us a professional railroaders what the ultimate goal entails. They have installed cameras at every corner, watching us and now they are invading our privacy over head. They are promoting the idea to all future workers to walk away with purpose. Please ban their gross use of drones.

Our company operates as a natural gas midstream operator and is classified as critical infrastructure.  The company has over 33,000 miles of pipeline and 3 LNG Sites with aboveground storage tanks. No-fly zones at the LNG sites would greatly enhance security at these sites.

The flying of the drones are unannounced especially the operators and thier motives. T&E employees are required to train for safety every year including but not limited to unoccupied bags / rigged devices safety breaches suspicious people as well as tresspasers,yet drones havnt been a topic. Whats the fine line between a armed drone or scout drone used to track rail cars and map out attacks vs mto or management just flying a drone to fail and scould a conductor. Safety priority security for the rail and its workers should be higher T&E should report everydrone like they would a suspicious vehicle or a group of people hanging in the woods at a 10mph speed restriction.

Aerotas submits this comment to urge the FAA to ensure that any final rule creating Unmanned Aircraft Flight Restrictions (“UAFRs”) preserves fast, low-friction access for lawful commercial UAS operations, especially surveying, mapping, inspection, engineering, and public-works operations.

Aerotas has helped land surveyors and civil engineers nationwide since 2014. We help firms convert drone-collected imagery, lidar, and high-precision GPS data into accurate maps, CAD deliverables, and 3D models. Our customers include small businesses, engineering firms, corporations, and firms working for public agencies on land development, roads, utilities, public works, power infrastructure, and other critical infrastructure.

UAS-enabled surveying reduces cost, improves data quality, improves safety, reduces field time, and shortens turnaround compared with traditional methods. These benefits depend on speed and predictability. Survey work is often constrained by weather, site access, crew availability, customer deadlines, and construction schedules. Regulatory delay is a real cost.

Aerotas recognizes that the FAA must address legitimate safety and security risks around sensitive fixed-site facilities. However, any UAFR system must be narrowly tailored and not convert lawful UAS operations into a slow discretionary process. A system that technically allows access, but requires unclear approvals, manual coordination, or delay, will become a de facto no-fly zone.

The proposed access pathway for Standard UAFRs is too narrow for mapping. The NPRM appears to permit Part 107 operators to access Standard UAFRs if they broadcast Remote ID, provide notice, and “transition” the UAFR in the shortest practicable time. That may work for pass-through flight. It does not work for surveying and mapping.
Surveying and mapping are not transitory operations. A typical survey requires multiple passes to obtain image overlap, lidar coverage, control verification, and data redundancy. Mapping missions may require grid flights, cross-grid flights, repeated passes, hovering, and low-altitude detail capture. These operations cannot satisfy a “shortest practicable transition” standard, even when safe, lawful, necessary, and conducted by certificated professionals.

The final rule should allow non-transitory commercial surveying, mapping, inspection, engineering, construction documentation, emergency repair, and public works within Standard UAFRs by qualified operators. At minimum, the FAA should permit an operator to remain within a Standard UAFR for the minimum time reasonably necessary to complete a lawful covered operation, rather than merely to “transition.”

For Standard UAFRs, the FAA deviations should be fast, reliable, and presumptively granted for lawful surveying and mapping absent a specific, material safety or security justification. Existing airspace authorization processes show that manual review and specific points of contact can make lawful airspace unavailable. Non-response, delay, or unjustified denial can create a practical no-fly zone. The FAA should not replicate that problem.

Aerotas recommends a LAANC-style process for Standard UAFR notice, access, and deviations. Qualified operators should be able to submit notice or request authorization through a centralized FAA system, receive near-real-time approval when objective safety criteria are satisfied, and operate without site-by-site discretion. Aerotas has no objection to requirements such as Part 107 certification, aircraft registration, Remote ID, and notice. But notice should be instant or near-instant, standardized, and centralized.

If the FAA requires case-by-case deviations, the final rule should include prompt response deadlines, deemed approval for non-response, written justification for any denial, and a clear appeal or cure pathway. For routine surveying and mapping, one or two business days should be the outside limit.

The final rule should also clarify asset-owner work. Asset owners often do not directly operate drones. For example, a utility may own a substation, hire an engineering firm, which hires a surveyor, which hires or uses a drone pilot. Even if the work is for the asset owner, the owner does not appear to have authority under the NPRM to grant a UAFR deviation; that authority remains with the FAA. Without a simple contractor pathway, projects may abandon UAS surveying and revert to slower, more expensive, and sometimes less safe methods.

Aerotas supports protecting genuinely sensitive facilities from unsafe or unlawful UAS activity. But Standard UAFRs should not create practical no-fly zones for lawful operations. Surveying and mapping are essential to infrastructure, construction, land development, utilities, and public works. The final rule should preserve the speed, predictability, and low-friction access that make UAS valuable.

Respectfully submitted,
Logan Campbell
President
Aerotas LLC

I have worked for the Railroad for 20 years and drone operations are unsafe.  Rail yards have enough moving parts without the added distraction of drones flying around to watch our every move. Not to mention my rail yard is a half a mile from a small airport.

My name is Bob Zednichek I am a 21 year railroad employee. I am writing you to address drone operation on the railroad and how I feel it may affect the safety of myself and those around me. Active railyards and rail operation are already dangerous enough with many other distractions around me to now have a possible flying distraction above my head to further distract me. I believe that overhead drones create another possible risk for myself and other employees as well as the public as I believe the railroad managers operate in a manner that they can do as they please regardless of what the frontline rail workers have to say as well as the cities, towns, and municipalities they operate in. Furthermore I believe it the use of drones over the thousands of gallons of dangerous chemicals we handle on an hour by hour and day to day basis should not be normalized for myself and fellow employees as well as the public. Lastly there is no way for a railroad professional to differentiate between a possible terrorist attack of individual seeking to cause harms to others and the railroad managers conducting tests.

I am a Conductor with 20 years of service we don’t need or want Drones in railyards!
-active rail yards are already dangerous enough without drones creating distractions overhead.
-drones are being used for surveillance and operational testing of that create unnecessary hazards and security concerns.
- railroad workers deserve to be consulted before these practices are normalized in critical infrastructure environments.
- normalizing drones flying over thousands of gallons of hazardous chemicals can't be allowed to happen, because we won't know when we are seeing an act of terrorism in progress vs. an MTO trying to conduct O-Tests.

Thank you

We already have thousands of rules to follow and then on top of that cameras in the locomotives, yards, and drones. Every yard in Portland is in the PDX flight path and these non trained drone operators are most certainly violating flight paths for PDX in the name of trying to fire as many employees as possible. It’s a safety hazard for the airlines as well as the railroad crews trying to do their job.

I am a switchman with 21 years of railroad experience, having hired out in 2005. Active rail yards are already dangerous enough without drones flying overhead creating additional distractions and stress for frontline workers.
It is one of the most disrespectful and stressful practices I’ve encountered from management. It is nearly impossible to stay focused on the job when you’re constantly looking up at the sky, knowing someone is trying to test or potentially fire you. This is completely uncalled for.
In the last 10 years, I have seen a sharp decline in job safety briefings. We might get one or two per year at best, and on the night shift there are zero. Instead of being present with us, management often observes from vehicles, cameras, or drones. That is not how safe railroad operations are conducted.
This practice makes our railroads more dangerous for both employees and the public. If management truly cared about our safety, they would conduct walk-alongs and critique our working practices side-by-side with us.
Additionally, with terrorist groups known to use drones, this creates serious security concerns. We work around thousands of gallons of hazardous materials every day. How are we supposed to tell the difference between a legitimate test and a real threat?
The solution is simple: this drone surveillance practice needs to end. We need more direct communication, regular job safety briefings, and hands-on walk-along training from management.
Frontline railroad workers are the real experts on rail safety. We deserve to be consulted before practices like this are normalized in critical infrastructure environments.

As a railroad switchman, the increased use of drones on my railroad has led to more distractions and privacy concerns, not only from managers but the public as well. Often there are no restrooms on our engines and while switching in the yard most conductors are forced to relieve themselves outdoors. On my railroad alone there has already been an incident where a conductor was relieving himself when a drone flew overhead to do an "observation" from a manager.
The distraction aspect has also been a problem as conductors need to constantly be aware of their surroundings and also listening for sounds that are uncommon to their normal switching. A drone overhead will lead to a potential distraction from a conductor, and could potentially cause harm to himself, equipment, or the general public if the commodity was a hazmat railcard.
Speaking of hazmat, the information that could be gained from the public flying over railyards or near the rails is alarming. Looking up UN numbers and often times the commodity is printed on the side of the car if it is a very dangerous car. In the wrong hands this information could be devastating to the general population as some derailed tank cars would have a 5 mile evacuation radius in the event of a derailment or spill.
Drones have no reason to be allowed near rail yards, either being flown from managers or the general public due to the above listed concerns. At the end of the day, ask yourself while you are at your job would you like a drone flown over you while you worked and how would it affect you?

As a nearly 20 year conductor in freight rail, I have experienced my share of close calls, near misses, and actual incidents. In every single one of them, being distracted from the task at hand was at least a contributing factor, if not the entire cause. Few things are more distracting than a drone buzzing over head while you try to work safely and efficiently. Allowing them to be used in a rail yard is a recipe for disaster.
Suggest that fire stations, ambulance stations and law enforcement stations be eligible for fixed site recognition and restrict UA in close proximity.
This is a great and much needed proposal. However, it should be extended to include transitory or temporary sites such as water/wastewater repair job sites.

commenter: Comment from Environmental Services, City of Portland
text:
To: Federal Aviation Administration / U.S. Department of Transportation
Subject: Request for FAA Drone Flight Restrictions Over Critical Wastewater Infrastructure Facilities in Portland, Oregon

I respectfully request consideration for establishing FAA drone flight restrictions over critical wastewater infrastructure facilities in Portland, Oregon, specifically our wastewater treatment plant operations and the Secondary Industrial Control System Operations (SICSO) facility. These facilities represent essential public utility infrastructure responsible for protecting public health, environmental compliance, regional water quality, and continuity of critical municipal services.

These sites are not simply industrial facilities; they are operationally sensitive critical infrastructure locations that support uninterrupted wastewater treatment, stormwater management, industrial control operations, and emergency response coordination for the region. Unauthorized or unregulated drone activity over these facilities introduces several significant risks, including:

Potential surveillance and reconnaissance of sensitive infrastructure systems, security layouts, access points, and operational processes.
Increased vulnerability to malicious activity targeting industrial control systems, electrical systems, communications infrastructure, and operational resiliency.
Safety hazards to personnel conducting field operations, maintenance activities, confined space work, crane operations, and emergency response functions.
Risks of drone interference with emergency operations, law enforcement activity, or mutual aid responses during critical incidents.
Disruption to operational continuity at facilities responsible for protecting waterways, public health, and environmental compliance requirements.
Elevated concern surrounding the growing use of unmanned aircraft systems near critical infrastructure nationwide and the increasing sophistication of drone capabilities.

Our wastewater treatment and SICSO operations support a broad network of critical sewer and stormwater infrastructure assets across the Portland metropolitan region. Disruption, surveillance, or interference affecting these facilities could have cascading impacts to environmental protection systems, public services, worker safety, and regional resiliency.

Additionally, these facilities already face ongoing operational and security challenges associated with unauthorized access attempts, vandalism, theft, and activity occurring around critical infrastructure corridors. Restricting unauthorized drone activity would provide an additional protective layer supporting both physical security and operational continuity.

Given the essential nature of wastewater and industrial control operations, we believe these facilities meet the intent of protecting sensitive critical infrastructure from unnecessary aerial exposure and operational risk. Establishing FAA drone restrictions or enhanced unmanned aircraft limitations over these facilities would significantly strengthen infrastructure protection efforts while supporting the safe and reliable delivery of essential public services.

Thank you for your consideration and continued efforts to protect critical infrastructure and public safety.

To Whom It May Concern,

I am submitting this comment anonymously as a frontline railroad conductor with well over a decade of experience working in active rail yards, terminals, and around moving trains. I am submitting anonymously because railroad employees must be able to raise safety concerns honestly without fear of retaliation.

I respectfully urge the FAA to place strong restrictions on unmanned aircraft systems, or drones, in and around active rail yards, rail terminals, switching operations, moving trains, and employees performing safety-sensitive railroad work.

Rail yards are not ordinary workplaces. Conductors and trainmen work on the ground around moving rail cars, locomotives, shove movements, switches, derails, close clearances, uneven walking conditions, radio communications, hazardous materials, and other employees. Rail cars can move very quietly, and serious injuries can happen in seconds. A momentary distraction can be the difference between going home safely and being seriously injured or killed.

Drones flown over or near active crews create an unnecessary and foreseeable distraction. When a drone is overhead, employees naturally look toward it, listen for it, react to it, wonder how close it is, or question why they are being watched. That attention is then taken away from the movement, the switches, the footing, the radio, and the equipment around them. In railroad operations, that is not a minor inconvenience; it is a safety hazard.

This is especially concerning when drones are used by railroad management for surveillance, operational testing, efficiency testing, or discipline. That type of use does not make the workplace safer. It adds another hazard to an already hazardous environment and pressures employees at the exact time they should be focused only on the job in front of them. Railroads already use fixed cameras, supervisor observation, rules testing, radio monitoring, and event recorder data. They do not need to fly drones over active crews to maintain accountability.

I am not opposed to every use of drones. There may be legitimate uses for emergency response, derailment documentation, infrastructure inspection, storm damage assessment, or security inspections when the operation is planned, controlled, communicated, and separated from employees actively working around moving equipment. But routine drone surveillance over crews performing live railroad work is different. The FAA should make that distinction clear.

The FAA should also make clear that terms such as “oversight,” “operational testing,” “efficiency testing,” or “management observation” should not become loopholes that allow drones to be normalized over employees performing safety-sensitive railroad work. If the purpose of the drone is to watch employees for discipline while they are switching cars or protecting movements, that is not a safety improvement. It is a new distraction placed directly into the work environment.

There is also a serious security concern. Rail yards and terminals often contain hazardous materials, tank cars, fuel, critical infrastructure, and key transportation routes. If drone activity becomes normal around rail facilities, employees on the ground may not know whether a drone overhead is being flown by a railroad supervisor, a contractor, an unauthorized operator, or someone with malicious intent. Normalizing drones over critical rail infrastructure makes it harder for employees to recognize and report a true security threat.

The workers on the ground are the true rail safety experts. We are the ones standing next to the equipment, lining switches, protecting shove movements, watching close clearances, and listening to radio instructions while cars are moving. We understand how quickly a routine move can become dangerous. Our attention belongs on the movement, not in the sky.

I respectfully ask the FAA to strengthen the proposed rule and prohibit drones from being used over or near active railroad employees for routine surveillance, operational testing, efficiency testing, or discipline. Any authorized drone operation inside an active rail facility should be limited to a legitimate safety, emergency, inspection, or security purpose and should require advance notice, clear communication, separation from active crews, and a written safety plan that addresses employee distraction, moving equipment, hazardous materials, and security concerns.

Railroad safety should be built around removing hazards, not adding new distractions above the heads of employees performing dangerous work. I respectfully urge the FAA to act before this practice becomes normalized and before an avoidable distraction contributes to a serious injury or fatality.

Respectfully submitted,

A frontline railroad conductor with well over a decade of experience
Submitted anonymously

I find it completely unnecessary for railroad companies to spy and regulate their ground employees via drone usage. It is a complete distraction for these employees to concentrate on the task at hand as well as do these tasks safely without being distracted with a drone buzzing around overhead. I also thinks it’s a safety risk to the general population around rail yards. Low flying planes and police helicopters seem to pose a risk to neighborhoods surrounding rail yards. Railroad employees are always subject to working on call and often with very short rest, they do not need this distraction to be concerned with, while performing dangerous tasks. Please ban railroad companies from being able to use drones to spy on employees, so these same employees can work safely.
I oppose the restriction of unmanned aircraft in close proximity to a fixed site facility. What defines close proximity? What defines an important fixed site?

As a railroad employee with over 30 years experience I would like to address the use of drones. I will keep my statement short with three main concerns:
1. They are a distraction plain and simple, an employee concerned about an over head drown in the worst sense can be struck and killed by rail equipment.
2. A defective drown can fall from the sky, placing employees and the public in danger.
3. Most rail yards are equipped with more than enough cameras (which are supposed to be for security) which should make drones unnecessary. If security is the main goal, access points should be the security focus.

commenter: Comment from Arena Management Holdings, Inc. dba Heritage Bank Center
organization: Arena Management Holdings, Inc. dba Heritage Bank Center
text: As Director of Safety and Security at Heritage Bank Center in Cincinnati, OH, we support the initiative to Restrict the Operation of Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed Site Facility. We bring more than 500,000 people to our facility annually over about 85 events per year. We are an indoor arena but experience line queues and other activity outside and unmanned aircraft in proximity present potential hazard to and disruption of our events.

It’s a double standard for railroads to say I can only stand or be so close to equipment without saying certain words or establishing physical protection from impact, but they feel it’s “safe” to fly drones over my head, fouling my airspace without any consideration for safety. We have to expect any train at any time in any direction, but drones can be used to foul my work zone with no protection?  It’s thinly veiled harassment through testing and nothing more. Working with a buzz knowing you’re going to get failed for something because that’s the drone team’s job, but nothing to do with safety at all.

I have been a railroader for 21 years. I have work yard, local and through freight during this time. I work environment is demanding and dynamic enough with normal hazards and obstacles that we do not need added hazards. I handle some of the most hazardous chemicals in the world in volumes that are truly terrifying.  Normalizing unmanned aerial vehicles around our yards and trains is a step too far, especially considering how easy it would be to weaponize one. We just have to look to Ukraine and Russia to see that. Also DGI dones have been banned, with current units grandfathered. Yet they present enough of a possible threat that they were banned. Should current ones be allowed around critical infrastructure for any reason? We do not the full extent of what china may be able to do with these drones and I for one would hate to imagine the possibilities. Im also an army veteran and I have a keen eye for physical security and this drone use is very worrisome. I ask that you rule for non governmental agencies to be barred their use around critical and hazardous infrastructure. Thank you

I disagree whole heartedly with the use of drone operations in our rail yards! As a switchman/ brakeman/ conductor/ & cut-back engineer, I work in these yards everyday. These drones are a major distraction from the safe work I am trying to preform. These things flying over your head divert your attention from the task at hand, and cause a very unsafe situation. Your focus is now on, What the heck is flying over me, and away from the move you are in the middle of making. Please rule these out of our rail yards!
Thank you,
Scott Lundberg

I’ve been a conductor for the past 15 years.
The majority of my time is spent in the yard performing switching operations.

Working in a railroad yard is not only stressful but dangerous as well. The last thing we need is another distraction taking our eyes and ears off of what we’re doing. That’s all a drone is, a distraction. It offers zero value to my job or the industry as a whole. An active rail yard is no place for a flying distraction piloted by an incompetent manager.

I have been a railroader for 21 years. I have work yard, local and through freight during this time. I work environment is demanding and dynamic enough with normal hazards and obstacles that we do not need added hazards. I handle some of the most hazardous chemicals in the world in volumes that are truly terrifying.  Normalizing unmanned aerial vehicles around our yards and trains is a step too far, especially considering how easy it would be to weaponize one. We just have to look to Ukraine and Russia to see that. Also DGI dones have been banned, with current units grandfathered. Yet they present enough of a possible threat that they were banned. Should current ones be allowed around critical infrastructure for any reason? We do not the full extent of what china may be able to do with these drones and I for one would hate to imagine the possibilities. Im also an army veteran and I have a keen eye for physical security and this drone use is very worrisome. I ask that you rule for non governmental agencies to be barred their use around critical and hazardous infrastructure. Thank you

I am a locomotive engineer with 21 years of service. Active rail yards are already dangerous enough without drones creating distractions overhead. Frontline railroad workers are the true experts on rail safety. These drones used for surveillance and operational testing create unnecessary hazards and security concerns. Railroad workers deserve to be consulted before these practices are normalized in critical infrastructure environments. Normalizing drones flying over the thousands of gallons of hazardous chemicals can't be allowed to happen, because us, as employees, won't know when we are seeing an act of terrorism in progress vs. a company official or MTO trying to conduct operations tests. Please restrict drone operations around transportation facilities!

This is not only a public hazard but an employee hazard. We have a company that is ridiculously infatuated with terminating employees so they can justify a one man crew. These drones, cameras, (in cabs, outside cabs, crew rooms, all over the yard, managers hiding in bushes, etc etc etc are creating a hostile environment. I’m to the point we’re I’m afraid to go to the bathroom at work in fear of cameras. As well as fear from camera in my hotel room. This has to stop!! It is nothing but an angry giant flexing its muscles against the common folk.

Re: Docket No. FAA-2026-4558; Notice No. 26-03
Designation: Restrict the Operation of Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed Site Facility

To the Federal Aviation Administration:

I submit this public comment as a railroader with thirty (30) years of railroad experience and as someone who reviews railroad discipline cases, including cases where drone footage has been used by railroad management. I support the FAA’s effort to address unmanned aircraft operations near critical infrastructure, including rail yards, chemical facilities, intermodal facilities, and other transportation-related locations.

Active rail yards are already dangerous enough. Railroad employees work around moving equipment, switching operations, hazardous materials, close clearances, live tracks, plant facilities, vehicles, contractors, and constant radio communication. The margin for error is thin. Adding drones into that environment creates another distraction, another uncertainty, and another hazard for the employees doing the work on the ground.

This is not hypothetical. Railroad managers have used drones around active rail operations and near plant facilities while crews were working. I am aware of situations where drone use raised questions from local law enforcement because the drone was flown near or over protected or sensitive facilities. I have also reviewed discipline matters where drone footage became part of the Carrier’s case against employees. That creates a serious safety culture problem. Employees are no longer focused only on the movement, job briefing, radio communication, close clearance, track conditions, or hazards around them. They are also looking skyward, wondering whether they are being watched from above.

That is not safety. That is distraction.

Railroad workers are the true rail safety experts. The employees switching cars, riding equipment, protecting shoves, handling hazardous materials, and working inside industrial plants understand these risks in a way that cannot be captured from a parking lot, drone camera, or management office. Drones should not be normalized as an operational testing or surveillance tool inside active rail yards without strong federal limits, clear safety requirements, and meaningful input from the workers affected.

The FAA should also consider the security risk. Railroad workers operate in and around critical infrastructure every day. Many rail yards and customer facilities handle hazardous chemicals, fuel, pressure vessels, and other sensitive materials. When drones fly over these locations as a normal management practice, employees on the ground have no reasonable way to know whether the drone is authorized, unauthorized, careless, or something more serious. A drone over rail infrastructure or chemical facilities should not be treated as routine.

If the FAA adopts this rule, I urge the agency to make clear that rail facilities, active yards, hazardous material handling locations, intermodal facilities, and rail-served industrial plants are environments where drone activity presents both safety and security risks. The rule should not create a loophole where a facility operator or railroad can restrict outside drones while freely using its own drones over employees without safety controls.

Any authorized drone use in or around active rail operations should include advance notice to affected crews when possible; restrictions against flying directly over active switching operations, moving trains, employees, or hazardous material handling except for documented safety necessity; coordination with affected frontline employees; clear identification of the drone operator and purpose; and limits on using drones as routine discipline or operational testing tools.

A drone does not become safe simply because it is flown by railroad management. The risk to employees is the same if the drone creates distraction, confusion, or uncertainty during safety-sensitive work. Railroaders are trained to keep their eyes and attention on the movement, track, equipment, crew members, and hazards around them. They should not be forced to split that attention because a manager is flying a drone overhead.

For these reasons, I respectfully urge the FAA to adopt strong restrictions on drone operations around rail infrastructure and to include specific worker-safety protections before drones are permitted or normalized in active railroad operating environments.

Respectfully submitted,

Charles Piland

Only way SMART can get anything done is if they stand their ground, we need the mafia to take over unions again!! The way we are being used by the railroad for work and extra moves is atrocious. The union now doesn’t take employees into heart, they only do what benefits them, why? Because they get paid anyways. We need a strong union that can fight for us. 8 moves in the yard for a road crew? Really? When did we become switchers? Grow a pair and stand up for us!!

The railroad is no place for drones no we have enough to look out for and don’t need drones flying over out heads and worrying about them looking down on us.  It has nothing to do with safety and everything do with trying to get mud in trouble

Greetings, as a pilot that holds an instrument rating and a railroad worker, I am writing you to STRONGLY support the proposed rule of limiting the use of drones around fixed site facilities, particularly rail yards.

As both a railroad conductor entering my fifth year in the industry and an FAA-certificated pilot with an instrument rating, I understand how important safety culture, situational awareness, and disciplined operations are in transportation environments.

Over the past several years, I have personally witnessed railroad management increasingly using drones inside active rail yards and around moving trains. These drones are often not being used for their intended purpose of inspecting bridges, infrastructure, or other hard-to-reach locations. Instead, they are being used to hover over crews and switching operations in an attempt to identify minor rule violations for discipline purposes.

Active rail yards are already dangerous enough environments without additional distractions flying overhead. Railroad workers are constantly monitoring train movement, radio communication, hand signals, track conditions, and hazardous surroundings. Introducing drones into these environments creates unnecessary distractions and undermines operational focus during critical tasks.

As a pilot, I also find this troubling from a safety and security perspective. In aviation, unmanned aircraft operations are treated seriously because they can create hazards, confusion, and loss of situational awareness if misused. Railroad management has normalized drones flying around trains carrying thousands of gallons of hazardous materials, often in close proximity to crews on the ground. That should concern everyone.

There is also a broader security issue. If drones become commonplace over rail infrastructure, workers may no longer immediately recognize when unauthorized or malicious drone activity is occurring. In an era where railroads remain critical national infrastructure, that normalization creates unnecessary risk.

Front-line railroad workers are the true experts on rail safety because we are the ones working in these environments every day. Before drone operations become permanently normalized throughout the railroad industry, the FAA should listen carefully to the concerns of the men and women on the ground.

I strongly support additional FAA restrictions on non-essential drone operations around active rail facilities and moving trains, especially when those drones are being used for surveillance and disciplinary purposes rather than legitimate infrastructure inspection.
This would make it very unsafe for the employees because they would be distracted.

The Government Should Reexamine the Assumptions.

Commentary on a flawed plan. The probability that this theoretical reactive fix will yield the desired results is very low. Most of what I see as a “solution” is predicated on products developed by and lobbied for by a now-banned Chinese toy company. Unfortunately, managers at the FAA were beguiled by existing "solutions" built into products mandated by the CCP.  Some of these “solutions” could be used against the US of A and our allies.   

The FAA gave representatives of that same company seats on committees that assisted them in promulgating policy and rules that compromised the safety of the US NAS and put domestic drone manufacturers at an economic disadvantage.  That relationship between representatives of the now-banned Chinese company and the government bordered on the absurd. The FAA selected DJI to be an LAANC SSP until some of us raised concerns that the FAA was giving them the “keys to the domestic airspace kingdom.” Furthermore, in some instances, DJI company representatives had more power than duly elected US politicians. Even more troubling is the notion that someone from the government purportedly shared classified material with DJI company representatives.

I would encourage the Federal government to use this opportunity to conduct a forensic study, including a FARA investigation, of the regulatory process, since the involvement of any CCP-sponsored companies in federal rulemaking or policy development.

The safety of the US National Airspace System is at stake.

I am submitting this comment as a surveyor with Welch Comer Engineers, Inc., a civil engineering and surveying firm working in Idaho and Washington. Our survey work supports public infrastructure, private development, utilities, transportation, construction, boundary work, design, and public agency projects.

UAS technology has become a practical field tool for surveyors, not just an optional add-on. Drone-based imagery and lidar allow us to collect safer, more complete site data in less time than many traditional methods. We use UAS data for topographic mapping, design surfaces, construction documentation, corridor mapping, utility-related work, and other engineering deliverables. These projects often have tight windows based on weather, access, construction schedules, traffic control, crew availability, and client deadlines.

Because of that, the approval process matters. If an operation is technically allowed but requires unclear coordination, manual review, or waiting on a specific person to respond, the work may still not realistically be completed. For a survey crew, delayed approval can mean losing a weather window, missing site access, rescheduling staff, delaying design, or using slower and sometimes less safe methods.

I support protecting truly sensitive facilities from unsafe or unlawful drone activity. However, the final UAFR rule should separate legitimate security concerns from routine professional work performed by qualified operators. A lawful, insured, Part 107 survey operation using registered aircraft, Remote ID, and standard safety procedures should not be treated the same as an unknown or careless flight near a sensitive facility.

The current concept of allowing access only to “transition” through a Standard UAFR does not fit surveying and mapping. Survey flights are usually not pass-through operations. A mapping mission may require grid flights, cross-grid flights, repeated passes, low-altitude detail shots, lidar coverage, photo overlap, control verification, and sometimes hovering or circling a feature to fully document it.

The final rule should allow qualified operators to conduct non-transitory surveying, mapping, inspection, engineering, construction documentation, emergency repair, and public works flights within Standard UAFRs when the operation is lawful and meets objective safety requirements. Operators should be allowed to remain within the area for the minimum time reasonably necessary to complete the covered professional operation.

The FAA should make the process centralized, predictable, and fast. A LAANC-style system would make sense for Standard UAFR access and deviations. Operators should be able to submit notice or authorization through one FAA system, document Part 107 certification, aircraft registration, Remote ID compliance, operating area, time window, and purpose of flight, and receive near-real-time approval when the operation meets defined criteria.

If case-by-case review is required, the final rule should include clear deadlines, written reasons for denial, a way to correct or resubmit an application, and deemed approval if there is no response within the required time. For routine survey and mapping work, one week should be the maximum approval time. Without those protections, delay or non-response can function the same as a denial.

The rule should also clearly address contractor work. In engineering and utility projects, the asset owner is often not the drone operator. A utility, public agency, or private owner may hire an engineering firm, and that firm may use an internal survey crew or a separate drone pilot. The final rule should provide a simple pathway so the operator performing the flight can obtain approval or provide notice without confusion over whether the asset owner must be directly involved.

UAS surveying is now part of how many engineering and survey firms deliver accurate, efficient, and safer project data. The final UAFR rule should protect sensitive sites without unintentionally blocking routine professional operations that support infrastructure, utilities, construction, land development, transportation, and public works.

I respectfully ask the FAA to preserve fast, predictable, and low-friction access for qualified commercial UAS operators performing surveying, mapping, inspection, engineering, construction documentation, and public works operations within Standard UAFRs.

Respectfully submitted,

Thomas Tucker
Survey Technician
Welch Comer Engineers, Inc.

Railroad engineer with 22 years of experience.

Active rail yards are already dangerous enough without drones creating distractions overhead.

Front line railroad workers are the true experts on rail safety.

Drones used for surveillance and operational testing create unnecessary hazards and security concerns.

Railroad workers deserve to be consulted before these practices are normalized in critical infrastructure environments.

Normalizing drones flying over the thousands of gallons of hazardous chemicals can't be allowed to happen, because we won't know when we are seeing an act of terrorism in progress vs. a manager trying to conduct O-Tests.

Transportation Dept:

My son works for Union Pacific and has experienced the distraction that drones cause in the rail yard. It is diverts their concentration from the task at hand (i.e.; verifying the safety of the train before each run, etc.). Please see that these unnecessary distractions do not remain in any rail work areas; before they cause loss of life due to their unnecessary distraction. Please pass the: Designation-Restrict the Operation of Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed Site Facility
A Proposed Rule by the Federal Aviation Administration on 05/06/2026 and keep all workers safe.

Thank you. Very concerned parent.

Subject: Support for the proposed Airspace Restriction Rule with Integrated Countermeasures
Dear I am writing to express my strong support for the proposed rule restricting the airspace up to 400 feet above Water and Wastewater facilities, with boundaries confined strictly within the facility’s proprietary lines. This measure is a critical step in ensuring the security, safety and privacy of these areas considering the global unrest and growing concerns and risks associated with Unauthorized Unmanned Aerial System (UAS) operations.
The prohibition of UAS operations within these designed airspace boundaries, except operators equipped with Remote Identification (Remote ID), is prudent and necessary approach. Most operators with Remote ID are identified, accountable and less prompt to unlawful or malicious drone activity.
But I advocate for the inclusion of counter measures within this regulation. This will allow Utility to use counter-drone technologies, such as system designed to capture or disable trespassing drones, will provide Utility the right to respond swiftly and effectively to the violation of their protected airspace and processes. Complementing these measures with authorized use of electronic frequency scrambling devices to interfere with video transmission and remote-control signals of unauthorized UAS will further enhance the capability to mitigate potential threats and prevent any trespassing.
Together, these provisions will empower Water and Wastewater Facility owners and operators to maintain control over their airspace, safeguard sensitive operations and protect the public and personnel from unintended disruptions or security breaches caused by unauthorized drone activity.
I urge the relevant authorities to carefully consider and adopt them in the final rule. The communities served by our sectors deserve those protections and it’s our duty to safeguard the effluent water of our process.
Thank you very much for your attention to this matter. And please do not hesitate to contact me if further discussion or clarification is required.

Sincerely,
Kouao-Eric Ekoue
Sr. Superintendent of Water Quality
Middlesex Water Company
Not only is a drone hovering over you while you are in the middle of a serious potentially dangerous job distracting but Union Pacific are using drones as a harassment and retaliation device!

To the Federal Aviation Administration, I am writing to bring to your attention a critical safety concern affecting railroad workers across the country. My name is David Rivera, and I work as a conductor on the extra board for Union Pacific in Tucson. I regularly work locals, yard assignments, and trains across routes in Arizona, New Mexico and Texas. Recently, we have experienced a significant conflict due to the use of drones by railroad management inside active railyards. These drones cause a serious distraction and interfere with our ability to maintain situational awareness—a critical factor when working with heavy equipment and hazardous, explosive, TIH, PIH chemicals and combustible material, and anything that can cause danger. It is the FAA's JOB and position to ensure that policies, procedures, and activities are safe for the American people. By not taking action against these unsafe drone operations, I feel that the agency is failing to uphold its mandate to protect critical infrastructure, you will not only fail the aviation field but you will now place yourself to blame for possible catastrophic consequences in Railway Freight.

commenter: Comment from Nevada Center for Applied Research Nevada UAS Test Site
organization: Nevada Center for Applied Research Nevada UAS Test Site
